tenors.narrative: Prediction markets show minimal price movement over the next day, with a median forecast of $77,028 (+0.1%), suggesting consensus for near-term consolidation. The interquartile range of $76,324–$77,732 indicates modest volatility expectations, with the 90th percentile at $78,748 reflecting limited upside tail risk across 30 markets.

One day later, markets price in slightly higher expectations at a $77,409 median (+0.6%), though confidence remains subdued as evidenced by a wider 10th percentile of $74,486. The 90th percentile reaches $79,808, suggesting modest probability of a near-2% rally, but the broader range indicates elevated uncertainty among prediction market participants.

By May 1st, prediction markets revert to near-spot expectations with a $77,057 median (+0.1%), despite substantially higher volume ($5.2M) indicating strong institutional participation. The 90th percentile capped at exactly $80,000 signals a soft ceiling in market expectations, with the 10th percentile at $74,038 implying roughly 10% downside risk over the one-month horizon.

tenors.narrative: Prediction markets expect a modest 7.3% decline in WTI to $95/bbl by end-April 2026, with moderate confidence reflected in a relatively tight interquartile range ($85-$105). The 10th-90th percentile spread ($79-$111) suggests reasonable consensus around a slight pullback from current levels.

Markets are pricing in a more significant 22.1% decline to $80/bbl by end-June 2026, though with considerably lower conviction given the wide dispersal in price expectations (10th-90th range of $61-$162). The substantial gap between median and 75th percentile ($80 vs. $134) indicates meaningful disagreement on longer-term oil dynamics, with notably lower trading volume supporting this tenor.
